title: Long-term exposure to ambient benzene and brain disorders among urban adults
methods: stratification by inflammation and metabolic syndrome

data_scope.benzene_grid_resolution: not explicitly stated in available text (DEFRA PCM national modeled surface)

core_conclusion: In a large UK urban cohort, long-term exposure to ambient benzene (per 0.30 ug/m3 IQR increment), even below 5 ug/m3, is associated with elevated risk of dementia (HR 1.18), major depression (HR 1.09) and anxiety disorder (HR 1.16). Neuroimaging linked exposure to the thalamus and superior temporal gyrus, giving population-level evidence that a common urban carcinogen also harms the brain.
why_it_matters: Extends a known carcinogen's harm to neurodegenerative and psychiatric outcomes; effects appear below current safe levels; pairs exposure with brain-imaging mechanism; squarely an urbanization problem as cities concentrate combustion sources.
why_nature: Couples a city-scale environmental exposure to brain health; large rigorous cohort (261,909 adults, up to 16-year follow-up) plus 23,911 imaged brains; mechanism plus outcome via neuroimaging and SEM; directly translatable to air-quality and health guidelines.
added_value_to_my_research: GeoAI fine-scale benzene exposure surface via multi-source fusion + explainable ML
Mobility-weighted personal exposure from GPS/mobile-phone activity spaces
Exposure-aware 2SFCA/R2SFCA accessibility to mental-health and dementia care
MGWR/GWR test of green-space and urban-form moderation of the benzene-brain link
Physics-informed graph model replacing linear SEM for exposure->structure->disorder
